addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring or one-time remote agent ("run this every Monday", "open a cleanup PR for X in 2 weeks"), or to manage existing routines. ALSO OFFER PROACTIVELY: after you finish work that has a natural future follow-up, end your reply with a one-line offer to schedule a background agent to do it. Strong signals: a feature flag / gate / experiment / staged rollout was just shipped (offer a one-time agent in ~2 weeks to open a cleanup PR or evaluate results), a new alert/monitor was created (offer a recurring agent to triage it), a TODO/migration with a "remove once X" condition was left behind (offer a one-time agent to do the removal). Skip the offer for refactors, bug fixes, and anything that is done once it ships. Name a concrete action and cadence ("in 2 weeks", "every Monday") and only offer when the run just succeeded — do not pitch a schedule for something that has not happened yet.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣A-
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
6	tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and dual Docker daemons.
7	
8	## Current Concept
9	
10	Architectural backbone in BUS0 (`Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUS0-BashUtilitiesSpec.adoc`).
11	Read BUS0 §Remote Node Access first; this paddock captures only state that
12	doesn't fit there.
13	
14	**Three regimes (BURN/BURP/BURW):**
15	
16	- BURN — node-shape (host, OS family, valid workload-template list).
17	  Per-node, git-transported, changes rarely. Identifier: viceroyalty.
18	- BURP — privileged credentials. Per-station-user, operator-authored.
19	  Identifier: investiture. References a viceroyalty via `burp_node`.
20	- BURW — workload state. Per-station-user, system-written by conscript.
21	  Identifier: lieutenancy. References a viceroyalty via `burw_node`.
22	
23	**Cross-cutting premises:**
24	
25	- `burn_host_singularity` — host of a node lives only in BURN; BURP and BURW
26	  reach it by reference, never duplicate.
27	- `bus_keys_operator_owned` — system never generates or modifies SSH key
28	  material; operator owns all key administration.
29	- single-admin-at-a-time — one privileged admin per node at a time;
30	  sequential transitions, not concurrent residence.
31	
32	**Verb vocabulary:**
33	
34	- Privileged tier: garrison, withdraw, conscript-{platform}, discharge,
35	  vanquish, inventory
36	- Workload tier: knock, remote_run, interactive_session
37

# jjezs_paddock ₣A-

## Goal

Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and dual Docker daemons.

## Current Concept

Architectural backbone in BUS0 (`Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUS0-BashUtilitiesSpec.adoc`).
Read BUS0 §Remote Node Access first; this paddock captures only state that
doesn't fit there.

**Three regimes (BURN/BURP/BURW):**

- BURN — node-shape (host, OS family, valid workload-template list).
  Per-node, git-transported, changes rarely. Identifier: viceroyalty.
- BURP — privileged credentials. Per-station-user, operator-authored.
  Identifier: investiture. References a viceroyalty via `burp_node`.
- BURW — workload state. Per-station-user, system-written by conscript.
  Identifier: lieutenancy. References a viceroyalty via `burw_node`.

**Cross-cutting premises:**

- `burn_host_singularity` — host of a node lives only in BURN; BURP and BURW
  reach it by reference, never duplicate.
- `bus_keys_operator_owned` — system never generates or modifies SSH key
  material; operator owns all key administration.
- single-admin-at-a-time — one privileged admin per node at a time;
  sequential transitions, not concurrent residence.

**Verb vocabulary:**

- Privileged tier: garrison, withdraw, conscript-{platform}, discharge,
  vanquish, inventory
- Workload tier: knock, remote_run, interactive_session

**Tabtarget colophons under `buw-`:**

- `buw-rn{l,r,v}` — BURN regime ops; analogous `buw-rp/rw{l,r,v}` for BURP/BURW
- `buw-np*` — privileged-tier verbs (np = uses privileged authority)
- `buw-nw*` — workload-tier verbs (nw = uses workload identity)
- `buw-hn0` — handbook landing for node ops

**BURS additions:**

- `BURS_KEY_DIR` — operator-managed SSH key directory (full path, no tilde)
- `BURS_WORKLOAD_STATE_DIR` — where BURW state files live (per-station-user)

## Heat Sequence

**Spec finalization (gates implementation):**

- reshape-spec-finalize — finish BUS0 verb table, cross-cutting principles
  section, subdoc consolidation

**Cleanup (depends on spec finalize):**

- AAf (scope-expanded) — delete deprecated BURN fields, residue prose,
  retired subdocs, legacy buhw handbook tabtargets, legacy
  `buw-rhc{l,m,x}` profile-minting if confirmed unused

**Investigation (independent, runs anytime):**

- AAe — localhost-fundus-parallel-saturation diagnosis

**Practice phase (Windows host) — blocked on yet-to-mint implementation paces:**

- AAD — WSL distro + Cygwin install
- AAE — JJK fundus inside WSL
- AAF — Docker dual-daemon

**Implementation paces:** TBD. Minted fresh after spec finalize + cleanup land.
Will cover garrison, withdraw, conscript-{platform}, discharge, vanquish,
BURS field additions, BURP/BURW regime tabtargets.

## Deferred (named for future return)

- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged trust.
  Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their own machine.
- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin scenarios.
  Pairs naturally with Adopt when both are needed.
- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't duplicate.

## Standing Notes

- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as
  `100.x.y.z` / tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency
- Windows username `b hyslop` (with space) — audit shell quoting at
  every `${BURP_USER}` expansion during practice
- Fundus capability registry: `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSFR-FundusRegistry.md`
- PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the
  Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call

# jjezs_pace ₢A-AAk

## Character
Spec-language: promote "jurisdiction" from implicit minting-prefix
side-effect to explicit named feature in BUS0. No code/kindle changes.

## Docket
The bujn-/bujp-/bujw- minting prefixes authored in ₢A-AAj name the
BURN/BURP/BURW family as "jurisdiction" by side-effect. BUS0 currently
carries no umbrella term for the trio; viceroyalty/investiture/lieutenancy
are the addressing rungs but the umbrella is unnamed.

Done when:
- BUS0 mapping section carries umbrella quoin(s) for the BURN/BURP/BURW
  family under "jurisdiction" framing.
- BUS0 § Remote Node Access section heading and overview text reflect
  the named frame; viceroyalty / investiture / lieutenancy stand as
  jurisdictional rungs.
- Paddock Current Concept three-regimes block names the umbrella.
- Asciidoc validates clean; no orphaned cross-references.

Out of scope: bujn-/bujp-/bujw- minting prefixes (treated as final);
kindle/CLI/tabtarget changes (no behavior change).

content.text:
Mounted **₣A- rbk-mvp-3-draft-windows-procedures** (11/21 done, racing).

**Paddock summary:** Architectural backbone for Windows test infrastructure lives in BUS0 §Remote Node Access. Three regimes — BURN (node-shape), BURP (privileged credentials), BURW (workload state) — addressed at rungs viceroyalty/investiture/lieutenancy. The trio currently has no umbrella name; the bujn-/bujp-/bujw- minting prefixes authored in prior pace ₢A-AAj implicitly call them "jurisdiction" but the spec doesn't name it.

**Recent work:** Just landed friendly-error UX for empty-channel commands (₢A-AAj final commit), preceded by BURN/BURP boilerplate storage migration and BUS0 spec reshape (drop deprecated fields, mint privilege regime quoins, rewrite Privilege Tiers).

**Next pace: ₢A-AAk name-jurisdiction-feature-in-spec [rough]**

Spec-language only — promote "jurisdiction" from implicit side-effect to named feature in BUS0. Done when:
- BUS0 mapping section carries umbrella quoin(s) under "jurisdiction" framing
- §Remote Node Access heading/overview reflect the named frame, with viceroyalty/investiture/lieutenancy as jurisdictional rungs
- Paddock three-regimes block names the umbrella
- AsciiDoc validates clean

**Name fit:** `name-jurisdiction-feature-in-spec` reads well — no relabel.

**Proposed approach:**
- Read BUS0 mapping section and §Remote Node Access to see current shape
- Mint umbrella quoin(s) for "jurisdiction" — likely a single `bus_jurisdiction` plus a small family (jurisdiction, jurisdictional rung) per Quoin Sub-Letter Discipline
- Rewrite §Remote Node Access heading + overview to introduce jurisdiction as the umbrella, recast viceroyalty/investiture/lieutenancy as jurisdictional rungs
- Update paddock Current Concept three-regimes block to name the umbrella
- Validate AsciiDoc clean, check for orphan cross-refs

**Execution:** Sequential opus — single spec file with quoin design judgment (sub-letter discipline, within-domain Y monosemy). The pace is rough and warrants a survey before edits to confirm the quoin shape fits BUS0's existing categories.

Proceed?

255	// end::mapping-section[]
256	
257	== Overview
258	
259	The Bash Utilities Specification defines the tabtarget dispatch system — the infrastructure for tab-completion-friendly launchers that route to kit formularies.
260	
261	BUK invented tabtargets; BUK defines them.
262	
263	For universal naming vocabulary (Cipher, Signet, Epithet, Inscription, Vesture), see VLS-VoxLiturgicalSpec.adoc.
264	
265	== Tabtarget Vesture
266	
267	[[bust_tabtarget]]
268	//axl_voices axo_entity
269	{bust_tabtarget}::
270	Vesture for tabtarget launcher identifiers.
271	Defines how launcher scripts in `tt/` are named.
272	+
273	* signet_case: lowercase with hyphen (the colophon)
274	* separator: `.`
275	* epithet_case: PascalCase (the frontispiece)
276	* envelope: `.sh`
277	+
278	Pattern: `{colophon}.{frontispiece}[.{imprint}].sh`
279	+
280	Examples:
281	+
282	* `rbw-cr.Rack.sh` — RBW workbench, Rack operation
283	* `rbw-cr.Rack.tadmor.sh` — same, with imprint specifying target
284	* `jjw-.HeatMount.sh` — JJK workbench, HeatMount operation
285	* `buw-tt.LauncherList.sh` — BUK tabtarget testbench, LauncherList operation
286	
287	== Dispatch
288	
289	The dispatch vocabulary defines the tabtarget launcher system.
290	A
291	{busdl_launcher}
292	validates arguments and delegates to a
293	{busdm_formulary}.
294	The
295	{busdc_colophon}
296	identifies the routing path;
297	the
298	{busdf_frontispiece}
299	provides human-readable description;
300	the
301	{busdi_imprint}
302	embeds target parameters.
303	
304	[[busdc_colophon]]
305	//axl_voices axt_string
306	{busdc_colophon}::
307	The routing identifier in a tabtarget inscription.
308	A
309	{busdc_colophon}
310	determines which
311	{busdm_formulary}
312	handles the dispatch.
313	Includes the hyphen as part of its identity (e.g., `rbw-`, `buw-tt-`).
314	+
315	The
316	{busdc_colophon}
317	is the first component of the tabtarget pattern, before the first dot separator.
318	+
319	Examples:
320	+
321	* `rbw-` routes to RBW workbench
322	* `jjw-` routes to JJK workbench
323	* `buw-tt-` routes to BUK tabtarget testbench
324	
325	[[busdf_frontispiece]]
326	//axl_voices axt_string
327	{busdf_frontispiece}::
328	The human-readable description in a tabtarget inscription.
329	A
330	{busdf_frontispiece}
331	uses PascalCase and describes the operation.
332	+
333	Appears after the
334	{busdc_colophon}
335	and dot separator.
336	+
337	Examples: `Build`, `Rack`, `RunVVX`, `HeatMount`.
338	
339	[[busdi_imprint]]
340	//axl_voices axt_string
341	{busdi_imprint}::
342	An optional embedded parameter in a tabtarget inscription.
343	An
344	{busdi_imprint}
345	specifies a target, instance, or configuration variant.
346	+
347	Appears after a second dot separator following the
348	{busdf_frontispiece}.
349	+
350	Examples:
351	+
352	* `tadmor` in `rbw-cr.Rack.tadmor.sh`
353	* `srjcl` in `rbw-cr.Rack.srjcl.sh`
354	
355	[[busdm_formulary]]
356	//axl_voices axo_entity
357	{busdm_formulary}::
358	A component that routes
359	{busdc_colophon_s}
360	to implementations.
361	Each
362	{busdm_formulary}
363	registers handlers for a set of
364	{busdc_colophon_s}
365	and dispatches to the appropriate function.
366	+
367	Types:
368	+
369	* **Workbench** (`{prefix}w_workbench.sh`): Routes commands for a kit
370	* **Testbench** (`{prefix}t_testbench.sh`): Routes tests for a kit
371	+
372	A
373	{busdm_formulary}
374	is sourced by the
375	{busdl_launcher}
376	and provides the dispatch function.
377	
378	[[busdl_launcher]]
379	//axl_voices axo_entity
380	{busdl_launcher}::
381	A bootstrap script in the `tt/` directory that validates arguments and delegates to a
382	{busdm_formulary}.
383	A
384	{busdl_launcher}
385	is the entry point for tabtarget invocation.
386	+
387	Naming follows
388	{bust_tabtarget}:
389	`{colophon}.{frontispiece}[.{imprint}].sh`
390	+
391	Responsibilities:
392	+
393	* Parse
394	{busdc_colophon}
395	from own filename
396	* Validate required arguments
397	* Source the target
398	{busdm_formulary}
399	* Invoke the dispatched function
400	
401	== Routing Behavior
402	
403	=== Dispatch Flow
404	
405	. User invokes launcher: `./tt/rbw-cr.Rack.tadmor.sh`
406	. Launcher parses its filename to extract:
407	* {busdc_colophon}:
408	`rbw-cr`
409	* {busdf_frontispiece}:
410	`Rack`
411	* {busdi_imprint}:
412	`tadmor`
413	. Launcher sources the appropriate
414	{busdm_formulary}
415	(`rbw_workbench.sh`)
416	. Formulary's dispatch function routes based on frontispiece
417	. Target function executes with imprint as parameter
418	
419	=== Workbench Pattern
420	
421	[source,bash]
422	----
423	# Tools/{kit}/{prefix}w_workbench.sh
424	
425	{prefix}w_dispatch() {
426	    local frontispiece="$1"
427	    local imprint="$2"
428	    shift 2
429	
430	    case "$frontispiece" in
431	        Build)          {prefix}w_build "$imprint" "$@" ;;
432	        Rack)           {prefix}w_rack "$imprint" "$@" ;;
433	        *)              buc_fatal "Unknown frontispiece: $frontispiece" ;;
434	    esac
435	}
436	----
437	
438	=== Testbench Pattern
439	
440	Testbenches follow the same pattern but route to test functions:
441	
442	[source,bash]
443	----
444	# Tools/{kit}/{prefix}t_testbench.sh
445	
446	{prefix}t_dispatch() {
447	    local frontispiece="$1"
448	    shift
449	
450	    case "$frontispiece" in
451	        Unit)        {prefix}t_unit "$@" ;;
452	        Integration) {prefix}t_integration "$@" ;;
453	        *)           buc_fatal "Unknown test: $frontispiece" ;;
454	    esac
455	}
456	----
457	
458	=== Test Registry Model
459	
460	The test registry (`butr_registry.sh`) uses two orthogonal enrollment dimensions:
461	
462	**Fixture** — init/setup execution context.
463	Each test case belongs to exactly one fixture.
464	Fixtures provide isolation via `_tsuite` subshell boundaries.
465	Registered with `butr_fixture_enroll(name, init_fn, setup_fn)`.
466	
467	**Suite** — cross-cutting selection group (N:M with cases).
468	A case can belong to multiple suites.
469	Suites classify by dependency tier (what environment must exist), not by test methodology.
470	A suite is a membership tag.
471	Suites enable suite dispatch: run a cross-cutting subset of cases grouped by their owning fixtures.
472	Suite context is set with `butr_suite_enroll(SUITE1 [SUITE2...])` before case enrollments.
473	Queried by `butd_run_suite(suite_name)`.
474	
475	Standard suite constants (defined as `readonly` in testbench kindle):
476	
477	* `BUTR_SUITE_FAST` — no external dependencies, CI-safe
478	* `BUTR_SUITE_SERVICE` — needs credentials, no container runtime
479	* `BUTR_SUITE_COMPLETE` — full test matrix including container runtime
480	
481	Dispatch entry points:
482	
483	* `butd_run_all()` — all fixtures, all cases
484	* `butd_run_fixture(name [case...])` — single fixture, optional case filter
485	* `butd_run_one(fn)` — single case with its fixture setup
486	* `butd_run_suite(suite)` — cross-cutting suite, grouped by fixture
487	
488	=== Colophon Conventions
489	
490	{busdc_colophon_s}
491	follow a structure that encodes both kit identity and formulary type:
492	
493	* `{cipher}-` — default workbench (e.g., `rbw-`, `jjw-`)
494	* `{cipher}-t-` — testbench (e.g., `buw-t-`, `jjw-t-`)
495	* `{cipher}-{variant}-` — specialized workbench (e.g., `rbw-cr-` for crucible rack operations)
496	
497	The trailing hyphen is always part of the colophon — it separates the routing prefix from the dot-delimited tabtarget components.
498	
499	== Test Invocation

:burx_log_hist:                   <<burx_log_hist,BURX_LOG_HIST>>
:burx_label:                      <<burx_label,BURX_LABEL>>
:burx_exit_status:                <<burx_exit_status,BURX_EXIT_STATUS>>
:burx_ended_at:                   <<burx_ended_at,BURX_ENDED_AT>>

// end::mapping-section[]

== Overview

The Bash Utilities Specification defines the tabtarget dispatch system — the infrastructure for tab-completion-friendly launchers that route to kit formularies.

BUK invented tabtargets; BUK defines them.

For universal naming vocabulary (Cipher, Signet, Epithet, Inscription, Vesture), see VLS-VoxLiturgicalSpec.adoc.

== Tabtarget Vesture

[[bust_tabtarget]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{bust_tabtarget}::
Vesture for tabtarget launcher identifiers.
Defines how launcher scripts in `tt/` are named.
+
* signet_case: lowercase with hyphen (the colophon)
* separator: `.`
* epithet_case: PascalCase (the frontispiece)
* envelope: `.sh`
+
Pattern: `{colophon}.{frontispiece}[.{imprint}].sh`
+
Examples:
+
* `rbw-cr.Rack.sh` — RBW workbench, Rack operation
* `rbw-cr.Rack.tadmor.sh` — same, with imprint specifying target
* `jjw-.HeatMount.sh` — JJK workbench, HeatMount operation
* `buw-tt.LauncherList.sh` — BUK tabtarget testbench, LauncherList operation

== Dispatch

The dispatch vocabulary defines the tabtarget launcher system.
A
{busdl_launcher}
validates arguments and delegates to a
{busdm_formulary}.
The
{busdc_colophon}
identifies the routing path;
the
{busdf_frontispiece}
provides human-readable description;
the
{busdi_imprint}
embeds target parameters.

[[busdc_colophon]]
//axl_voices axt_string
{busdc_colophon}::
The routing identifier in a tabtarget inscription.
A
{busdc_colophon}
determines which
{busdm_formulary}
handles the dispatch.
Includes the hyphen as part of its identity (e.g., `rbw-`, `buw-tt-`).
+
The
{busdc_colophon}
is the first component of the tabtarget pattern, before the first dot separator.
+
Examples:
+
* `rbw-` routes to RBW workbench
* `jjw-` routes to JJK workbench
* `buw-tt-` routes to BUK tabtarget testbench

[[busdf_frontispiece]]
//axl_voices axt_string
{busdf_frontispiece}::
The human-readable description in a tabtarget inscription.
A
{busdf_frontispiece}
uses PascalCase and describes the operation.
+
Appears after the
{busdc_colophon}
and dot separator.
+
Examples: `Build`, `Rack`, `RunVVX`, `HeatMount`.

[[busdi_imprint]]
//axl_voices axt_string
{busdi_imprint}::
An optional embedded parameter in a tabtarget inscription.
An
{busdi_imprint}
specifies a target, instance, or configuration variant.
+
Appears after a second dot separator following the
{busdf_frontispiece}.
+
Examples:
+
* `tadmor` in `rbw-cr.Rack.tadmor.sh`
* `srjcl` in `rbw-cr.Rack.srjcl.sh`

[[busdm_formulary]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busdm_formulary}::
A component that routes
{busdc_colophon_s}
to implementations.
Each
{busdm_formulary}
registers handlers for a set of
{busdc_colophon_s}
and dispatches to the appropriate function.
+
Types:
+
* **Workbench** (`{prefix}w_workbench.sh`): Routes commands for a kit
* **Testbench** (`{prefix}t_testbench.sh`): Routes tests for a kit
+
A
{busdm_formulary}
is sourced by the
{busdl_launcher}
and provides the dispatch function.

[[busdl_launcher]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busdl_launcher}::
A bootstrap script in the `tt/` directory that validates arguments and delegates to a
{busdm_formulary}.
A
{busdl_launcher}
is the entry point for tabtarget invocation.
+
Naming follows
{bust_tabtarget}:
`{colophon}.{frontispiece}[.{imprint}].sh`
+
Responsibilities:
+
* Parse
{busdc_colophon}
from own filename
* Validate required arguments
* Source the target
{busdm_formulary}
* Invoke the dispatched function

== Routing Behavior

=== Dispatch Flow

. User invokes launcher: `./tt/rbw-cr.Rack.tadmor.sh`
. Launcher parses its filename to extract:
* {busdc_colophon}:
`rbw-cr`
* {busdf_frontispiece}:
`Rack`
* {busdi_imprint}:
`tadmor`
. Launcher sources the appropriate
{busdm_formulary}
(`rbw_workbench.sh`)
. Formulary's dispatch function routes based on frontispiece
. Target function executes with imprint as parameter

=== Workbench Pattern

[source,bash]
----
# Tools/{kit}/{prefix}w_workbench.sh

{prefix}w_dispatch() {
    local frontispiece="$1"
    local imprint="$2"
    shift 2

    case "$frontispiece" in
        Build)          {prefix}w_build "$imprint" "$@" ;;
        Rack)           {prefix}w_rack "$imprint" "$@" ;;
        *)              buc_fatal "Unknown frontispiece: $frontispiece" ;;
    esac
}
----

=== Testbench Pattern

Testbenches follow the same pattern but route to test functions:

[source,bash]
----
# Tools/{kit}/{prefix}t_testbench.sh

{prefix}t_dispatch() {
    local frontispiece="$1"
    shift

    case "$frontispiece" in
        Unit)        {prefix}t_unit "$@" ;;
        Integration) {prefix}t_integration "$@" ;;
        *)           buc_fatal "Unknown test: $frontispiece" ;;
    esac
}
----

=== Test Registry Model

The test registry (`butr_registry.sh`) uses two orthogonal enrollment dimensions:

**Fixture** — init/setup execution context.
Each test case belongs to exactly one fixture.
Fixtures provide isolation via `_tsuite` subshell boundaries.
Registered with `butr_fixture_enroll(name, init_fn, setup_fn)`.

**Suite** — cross-cutting selection group (N:M with cases).
A case can belong to multiple suites.
Suites classify by dependency tier (what environment must exist), not by test methodology.
A suite is a membership tag.
Suites enable suite dispatch: run a cross-cutting subset of cases grouped by their owning fixtures.
Suite context is set with `butr_suite_enroll(SUITE1 [SUITE2...])` before case enrollments.
Queried by `butd_run_suite(suite_name)`.

Standard suite constants (defined as `readonly` in testbench kindle):

* `BUTR_SUITE_FAST` — no external dependencies, CI-safe
* `BUTR_SUITE_SERVICE` — needs credentials, no container runtime
* `BUTR_SUITE_COMPLETE` — full test matrix including container runtime

Dispatch entry points:

* `butd_run_all()` — all fixtures, all cases
* `butd_run_fixture(name [case...])` — single fixture, optional case filter
* `butd_run_one(fn)` — single case with its fixture setup
* `butd_run_suite(suite)` — cross-cutting suite, grouped by fixture

=== Colophon Conventions

{busdc_colophon_s}
follow a structure that encodes both kit identity and formulary type:

* `{cipher}-` — default workbench (e.g., `rbw-`, `jjw-`)
* `{cipher}-t-` — testbench (e.g., `buw-t-`, `jjw-t-`)
* `{cipher}-{variant}-` — specialized workbench (e.g., `rbw-cr-` for crucible rack operations)

The trailing hyphen is always part of the colophon — it separates the routing prefix from the dot-delimited tabtarget components.

== Test Invocation

942	=== BURN Regime
943	
944	[[burn_regime]]
945	//axvr_regime axf_bash axrd_file_sourced
946	{burn_regime}::
947	Per-node configuration regime describing the shape of a remote target.
948	A
949	{burn_regime}
950	instance is identified by its
951	{burn_viceroyalty}
952	(directory name) and is shared across station users (git-transported);
953	it carries no key material and changes rarely.
954	+
955	{burn_regime}
956	files live under `.buk/rbmn_nodes/<{burn_viceroyalty}>/burn.env`.
957	+
958	{burn_regime}
959	is the sole home of node address-of-record per
960	{burn_host_singularity};
961	{burp_regime}
962	and
963	{burw_regime}
964	instances reference it via
965	{burp_node}
966	and
967	{burw_node}.
968	
969	[[burn_host]]
970	//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string
971	{burn_host}::
972	IP address or hostname of the remote node.
973	
974	[[burn_platform]]
975	//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_xname
976	{burn_platform}::
977	OS family of the node.
978	Selects procedure dispatch for OS-specific ceremonies
979	(garrison shell selection, sshd configuration paths, quoting regime).
980	Values: `linux`, `mac`, `windows`.
981	+
982	Admin-shell variant on Windows nodes (cygwin / wsl / powershell) is
983	captured in
984	{burp_command}
985	per investiture, not at the node level — a single Windows host can
986	support multiple admin-shell stacks under separate
987	{burp_investiture}
988	instances.
989	Workload-shell variant is captured in
990	{burw_lieutenancy}
991	naming.
992	
993	[[burn_workloads]]
994	//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string axd_repeated
995	{burn_workloads}::
996	Authoritative template of valid
997	{burw_lieutenancy}
998	strings on this node.
999	{busn_conscript}
1000	may only materialize a workload whose alias is enumerated here;
1001	the operator pre-declares the node's supported workload offices.
1002	
1003	=== BURN/BURP/BURW Identifier Vocabulary
1004	
1005	The following quoins establish the minted-name vocabulary for the
1006	BURN/BURP/BURW model.
1007	
1008	[[burn_viceroyalty]]
1009	//axl_voices axo_entity
1010	{burn_viceroyalty}::
1011	The minted identifier of a
1012	{burn_regime}
1013	instance.
1014	{burp_regime}
1015	and
1016	{burw_regime}
1017	instances reference a node by its
1018	{burn_viceroyalty}.
1019	+
1020	The
1021	{burn_viceroyalty}
1022	is conveyed by the directory containing the
1023	{burn_regime}
1024	assignment file;
1025	it is not redundantly inscribed as a field within the file.
1026	+
1027	Examples: `redbox`, `winbox-prod`, `hawthorne`.
1028	
1029	[[burp_investiture]]
1030	//axl_voices axo_entity
1031	{burp_investiture}::
1032	The minted identifier of a
1033	{burp_regime}
1034	instance — the operator's named grant of privileged authority over a
1035	{burn_viceroyalty}.
1036	+
1037	The
1038	{burp_investiture}
1039	is conveyed by the directory containing the
1040	{burp_regime}
1041	assignment file;
1042	it is not redundantly inscribed as a field within the file.
1043	+
1044	Examples: `redbox-bhyslop-admin`, `winbox-prod-alice-admin`.
1045	
1046	[[burw_lieutenancy]]
1047	//axl_voices axo_entity
1048	{burw_lieutenancy}::
1049	The minted identifier of a
1050	{burw_regime}
1051	instance — a workload office operating on a
1052	{burn_viceroyalty}
1053	under the authority of a
1054	{burp_investiture}.
1055	A
1056	{burw_lieutenancy}
1057	names a workload entry (shell command, remote OS user, key file) that
1058	materializes when a workload is conscripted.
1059	+
1060	The
1061	{burw_lieutenancy}
1062	is conveyed by the directory containing the
1063	{burw_regime}
1064	state file;
1065	it is not redundantly inscribed as a field within the file.
1066	+
1067	The
1068	{burw_lieutenancy}
1069	string is by construction the remote OS username of its workload
1070	account on the node, drawn at conscript time from the
1071	{burn_viceroyalty}'s authoritative `burn_workloads` template.
1072	+
1073	Examples: `redbox-cyg`, `redbox-wsl`, `redbox-pwsh`.
1074	
1075	[[burn_host_singularity]]
1076	//axl_voices axk_premise
1077	{burn_host_singularity}::
1078	The host of a node lives only in
1079	{burn_regime}.
1080	{burp_regime}
1081	and
1082	{burw_regime}
1083	instances reach the host by reference
1084	(via
1085	{burp_node}
1086	and
1087	{burw_node}
1088	respectively) and never duplicate it.
1089	A given node has exactly one address-of-record;
1090	credentials and workload state never re-state it.
1091	
1092	[[bus_keys_operator_owned]]
1093	//axl_voices axk_premise
1094	{bus_keys_operator_owned}::
1095	The system never generates or modifies SSH key material.
1096	The operator owns all key administration: keypair creation,
1097	distribution of pubkeys into regime files, and placement of private
1098	keys on the operator's station.
1099	Regime files carry only the public-key line;
1100	private keys live at operator-managed paths and are never written by
1101	BUK procedures.
1102	
1103	=== BURP Regime
1104	
1105	[[burp_prefix]]
1106	//axl_voices axrg_prefix
1107	{burp_prefix}::
1108	Bash Utility Regime Privileged
1109	
1110	[[burp_regime]]
1111	//axvr_regime axf_bash axrd_file_sourced
1112	{burp_regime}::
1113	Per-station-user privileged-credential regime.
1114	A
1115	{burp_regime}
1116	instance is identified by its
1117	{burp_investiture}
1118	(directory name).
1119	+
1120	{burp_regime}
1121	files live under
1122	`.buk/rbmu_users/<{burs_user}>/<{burp_investiture}>/burp.env`.
1123	+
1124	{burp_regime}
1125	references its node by
1126	{burp_node}
1127	per
1128	{burn_host_singularity}.
1129	
1130	[[burp_node]]
1131	//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_xname
1132	{burp_node}::
1133	Reference to a
1134	{burn_viceroyalty}
1135	— the node this investiture is granted over.
1136	Resolves to the
1137	{burn_regime}
1138	file at the directory bearing this name.
1139	
1140	[[burp_user]]
1141	//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string
1142	{burp_user}::
1143	Remote admin OS user this credential authenticates as
1144	(e.g., `Administrator` on Windows, `root` or a sudo-capable user on
1145	Linux/Mac).
1146	
1147	[[burp_ssh_pubkey]]
1148	//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string
1149	{burp_ssh_pubkey}::
1150	Full SSH public key line of the operator-managed admin keypair.
1151	The matching private key lives at `${BURS_KEY_DIR}/<{burp_investiture}>` on
1152	the operator's station per
1153	{bus_keys_operator_owned}.
1154	
1155	[[burp_key_file]]
1156	//axvr_variable axd_optional axtu_string
1157	{burp_key_file}::
1158	Operator-managed SSH private key filename.
1159	Resolves under the operator's key directory (typically `~/.ssh/`) per
1160	{bus_keys_operator_owned}.
1161	Empty defaults to the
1162	{burp_investiture}
1163	(one key per investiture).
1164	
1165	[[burp_command]]
1166	//axvr_variable axd_optional axtu_string
1167	{burp_command}::
1168	Shell command for SSH `command=` routing in the node's
1169	`administrators_authorized_keys`.
1170	Empty for direct-shell connections;
1171	non-empty when the admin shell is selected at the SSH layer
1172	(e.g., `powershell.exe`, `C:\cygwin64\bin\bash.exe -l`).
1173	
1174	=== BURW Regime
1175	
1176	[[burw_prefix]]
1177	//axl_voices axrg_prefix
1178	{burw_prefix}::
1179	Bash Utility Regime Workload
1180	
1181	[[burw_regime]]

(garrison shell selection, sshd configuration paths, quoting regime).

+

=== BURN/BURP/BURW Identifier Vocabulary

The following quoins establish the minted-name vocabulary for the

[[burn_viceroyalty]]

{burn_regime}

[[burp_investiture]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{burp_investiture}::
The minted identifier of a
{burp_regime}
instance — the operator's named grant of privileged authority over a
{burn_viceroyalty}.
+
The
{burp_investiture}
is conveyed by the directory containing the
{burp_regime}
assignment file;
it is not redundantly inscribed as a field within the file.
+
Examples: `redbox-bhyslop-admin`, `winbox-prod-alice-admin`.

[[burw_lieutenancy]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{burw_lieutenancy}::
The minted identifier of a
{burw_regime}
instance — a workload office operating on a
{burn_viceroyalty}
under the authority of a
{burp_investiture}.
A
{burw_lieutenancy}
names a workload entry (shell command, remote OS user, key file) that
materializes when a workload is conscripted.
+
The
{burw_lieutenancy}
is conveyed by the directory containing the
{burw_regime}
state file;
it is not redundantly inscribed as a field within the file.
+
The
{burw_lieutenancy}
string is by construction the remote OS username of its workload
account on the node, drawn at conscript time from the
{burn_viceroyalty}'s authoritative `burn_workloads` template.
+
Examples: `redbox-cyg`, `redbox-wsl`, `redbox-pwsh`.

[[burn_host_singularity]]
//axl_voices axk_premise
{burn_host_singularity}::
The host of a node lives only in
{burn_regime}.
{burp_regime}
and
{burw_regime}
instances reach the host by reference
(via
{burp_node}
and
{burw_node}
respectively) and never duplicate it.
A given node has exactly one address-of-record;
credentials and workload state never re-state it.

[[bus_keys_operator_owned]]
//axl_voices axk_premise
{bus_keys_operator_owned}::
The system never generates or modifies SSH key material.
The operator owns all key administration: keypair creation,
distribution of pubkeys into regime files, and placement of private
keys on the operator's station.
Regime files carry only the public-key line;
private keys live at operator-managed paths and are never written by
BUK procedures.

=== BURP Regime

[[burp_prefix]]
//axl_voices axrg_prefix
{burp_prefix}::
Bash Utility Regime Privileged

[[burp_regime]]
//axvr_regime axf_bash axrd_file_sourced
{burp_regime}::
Per-station-user privileged-credential regime.
A
{burp_regime}
instance is identified by its
{burp_investiture}
(directory name).
+
{burp_regime}
files live under
`.buk/rbmu_users/<{burs_user}>/<{burp_investiture}>/burp.env`.
+
{burp_regime}
references its node by
{burp_node}
per
{burn_host_singularity}.

[[burp_node]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_xname
{burp_node}::
Reference to a
{burn_viceroyalty}
— the node this investiture is granted over.
Resolves to the
{burn_regime}
file at the directory bearing this name.

[[burp_user]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string
{burp_user}::
Remote admin OS user this credential authenticates as
(e.g., `Administrator` on Windows, `root` or a sudo-capable user on
Linux/Mac).

[[burp_ssh_pubkey]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string
{burp_ssh_pubkey}::
Full SSH public key line of the operator-managed admin keypair.
The matching private key lives at `${BURS_KEY_DIR}/<{burp_investiture}>` on
the operator's station per
{bus_keys_operator_owned}.

[[burp_key_file]]
//axvr_variable axd_optional axtu_string
{burp_key_file}::
Operator-managed SSH private key filename.
Resolves under the operator's key directory (typically `~/.ssh/`) per
{bus_keys_operator_owned}.
Empty defaults to the
{burp_investiture}
(one key per investiture).

[[burp_command]]
//axvr_variable axd_optional axtu_string
{burp_command}::
Shell command for SSH `command=` routing in the node's
`administrators_authorized_keys`.
Empty for direct-shell connections;
non-empty when the admin shell is selected at the SSH layer
(e.g., `powershell.exe`, `C:\cygwin64\bin\bash.exe -l`).

=== BURW Regime

[[burw_prefix]]
//axl_voices axrg_prefix
{burw_prefix}::
Bash Utility Regime Workload

[[burw_regime]]

== Remote Node Access

Bringing remote nodes under SSH key control from a local station, establishing
trust that supports both privileged node-level operations and ephemeral
workload-tier work over the same {burn_regime}.

=== Privilege Tiers

Privilege is encoded by regime, not by a field on
{burn_regime}.
Each
{burp_regime}
instance grants a single station user privileged authority over a
{burn_viceroyalty},
and each
{burw_regime}
instance records an ephemeral workload user materialized on that
viceroyalty by
{busn_conscript}.
Tabtargets dispatch by colophon family
(`buw-np*` for privileged-tier verbs,
`buw-nw*` for workload-tier verbs)
and consume the regime appropriate to the verb.

==== Privileged Tier

Privileged operations authenticate as a remote OS user with admin or
equivalent privileges via a
{burp_regime}
instance.
Used for node-level operations: sshd configuration, user provisioning,
node-establishment ceremonies.

Multiple station users may hold privileged trust on the same node (file
format supports multi-resident keys). Operational convention: only one
station user actively orchestrates work at a time. This convention is
enforced by social contract, not BUK locking.

==== Workload Tier

Workload operations authenticate as ephemeral remote OS users created by
privileged authority and tracked by a
{burw_regime}
instance.
Used for normal remote work: running commands, file operations,
interactive shells.

Workload users are exclusive to one station user — not handed off between
station users. Lifecycle is short:
{busn_conscript}
when needed,
{busn_discharge}
when done.

=== Tabtarget Catalog

Tabtargets under `buw-` consume {burn_regime} profiles to perform remote
operations. Tier-specific colophons follow `buw-n[pw]` for clarity.

==== Lifecycle

[[bust_node_regime_list]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_node_regime_list}::
`buw-rnl` —
List all {burn_regime} profiles under `.buk/rbmn_nodes/`.

[[bust_node_regime_render]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_node_regime_render}::
`buw-rnr` —
Render a single {burn_regime} profile (viceroyalty passed as positional argument).

[[bust_node_regime_validate]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_node_regime_validate}::
`buw-rnv` —
Validate a single {burn_regime} profile (viceroyalty passed as positional argument).

[[bust_privilege_regime_list]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_privilege_regime_list}::
`buw-rpl` —
List all {burp_regime} profiles for the current {burs_user}.

[[bust_privilege_regime_render]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_privilege_regime_render}::
`buw-rpr` —
Render a single {burp_regime} profile (investiture passed as positional argument).

[[bust_privilege_regime_validate]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_privilege_regime_validate}::
`buw-rpv` —
Validate a single {burp_regime} profile (investiture passed as positional argument).

==== Privileged (per-platform node setup)

[[bust_garrison_linux]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_garrison_linux}::
`buw-npgl` —
{busn_garrison} ceremony for Linux nodes.

[[bust_garrison_mac]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_garrison_mac}::
`buw-npgm` —
{busn_garrison} ceremony for macOS nodes.

[[bust_garrison_cygwin]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_garrison_cygwin}::
`buw-npgc` —
{busn_garrison} ceremony for Cygwin nodes.

[[bust_garrison_wsl]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_garrison_wsl}::
`buw-npgw` —
{busn_garrison} ceremony for WSL nodes.

[[bust_garrison_powershell]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_garrison_powershell}::
`buw-npgp` —
{busn_garrison} ceremony for PowerShell nodes.

[[bust_garrison_localhost]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_garrison_localhost}::
`buw-npgx` —
{busn_garrison} ceremony for localhost nodes.

==== Workload (privileged authority required)

[[bust_conscript_linux]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_conscript_linux}::
`buw-nwcl` —
{busn_conscript} a workload user with a Linux shell, via the named privileged BURN.

[[bust_conscript_mac]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_conscript_mac}::
`buw-nwcm` —
{busn_conscript} a workload user with a macOS shell, via the named privileged BURN.

[[bust_conscript_cygwin]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_conscript_cygwin}::
`buw-nwcc` —
{busn_conscript} a workload user with a Cygwin shell, via the named privileged BURN.

[[bust_conscript_wsl]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_conscript_wsl}::
`buw-nwcw` —
{busn_conscript} a workload user with a WSL shell, via the named privileged BURN.

[[bust_conscript_powershell]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_conscript_powershell}::
`buw-nwcp` —
{busn_conscript} a workload user with a PowerShell shell, via the named privileged BURN.

[[bust_conscript_localhost]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_conscript_localhost}::
`buw-nwcx` —
{busn_conscript} a workload user with a localhost shell, via the named privileged BURN.

[[bust_discharge]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_discharge}::
`buw-nwd` —
{busn_discharge} a workload user.
Deletes the remote OS user via the workload profile's privileged
authority and removes the local profile.

[[bust_inventory]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_inventory}::
`buw-nwi` —
{busn_inventory} workload users present on the node, queried via the
privileged BURN.

==== Workload (operational)

[[bust_knock]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_knock}::
`buw-nwk` —
Probe workload responsiveness via passwordless SSH.

[[bust_remote_run]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_remote_run}::
`buw-nwr` —
Run the contents of a command file on the remote node; capture output to
{burd_output_dir}.

[[bust_interactive_session]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_interactive_session}::
`buw-nws` —
Open an interactive SSH session to the workload account.

==== Handbook

[[bust_handbook_node_top]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_handbook_node_top}::
`buw-hn0` —
Top-level handbook landing for node access procedures.

=== Privileges

A
{burn_regime}
instance lives under `.buk/rbmn_nodes/<viceroyalty>/` and is shared
across station users (git-transported); it carries node shape only and
no key material.
A
{burp_regime}
instance lives under `.buk/rbmu_users/<burs_user>/<investiture>/`;
the
{burs_user}
owns its privileged-credential subtree exclusively, and operations
write only under the current station user's tree.

Each platform subdoc declares the remote-user privilege contract that its
ceremony assumes (e.g., on Windows the privileged user must be a member of
the Administrators group).

=== Verb Definitions

[[busn_garrison]]
//axl_voices axt_command
{busn_garrison}::
Establish privileged BURN trust on a node by writing the privileged
{burn_regime} profile and running the platform-specific ceremony to gain
admin access. Per-platform tabtargets at `buw-npg{platform}`. First-time
node setup; idempotent on re-run.

[[busn_conscript]]
//axl_voices axt_command
{busn_conscript}::
Create an ephemeral workload user on a node and provision a workload
{burn_regime} profile locally. Requires a privileged BURN as authority for
the remote-user creation. Per-platform tabtargets at `buw-nwc{platform}`,
where the platform letter selects the workload shell — independent of the
privileged BURN's shell choice.

[[busn_discharge]]
//axl_voices axt_command
{busn_discharge}::
Remove a workload user from a node and delete the local workload
{burn_regime} profile. The workload profile encodes its privileged
authority; {busn_discharge} uses that authority to delete the remote OS
user. Tabtarget: `buw-nwd`.

[[busn_inventory]]

content.text:
Survey complete. Here is my intended change for ₢A-AAk before any edits.

---

## Quoin design

Mint **two** quoins under the existing `bus_` cross-cutting category (which today houses `bus_keys_operator_owned`, the only other `bus_` quoin — both serve the same role: cross-regime concepts):

- **`bus_jurisdiction`** — voicing `axl_voices axo_entity` — the umbrella concept naming the BURN/BURP/BURW family as a single coordinated authority model over a remote node.
- **`bus_jurisdictional_rung`** — voicing `axl_voices axo_entity` — the addressing-level term; viceroyalty / investiture / lieutenancy each instantiate a rung within a jurisdiction.

Rationale for two: the docket says "umbrella quoin(s)" (plural-allowed). One umbrella alone leaves "rungs" as plain prose; the rung concept is load-bearing because it's what binds the three identifiers into a system. Two quoins give us the noun for the family AND the role-name for its members, which is exactly the addressing vocabulary the docket asks us to make explicit.

If you'd prefer just `bus_jurisdiction` and let "rungs of the jurisdiction" stay as prose, say so — I'll trim.

## Edits in BUS0

1. **Mapping-section comment** (lines ~4–32): add `// bus_:   BUK Cross-cutting (premises and umbrella concepts spanning regimes)` to the category-declarations comment block (currently missing — `bus_keys_operator_owned` is uncategorized today).

2. **Mapping-section attribute references** (near line ~127, the existing "Cross-cutting premises for BURN/BURP/BURW" block): add the two new `:bus_jurisdiction:` / `:bus_jurisdictional_rung:` attribute references. Rename the comment to read "Cross-cutting jurisdiction concepts" so the block now hosts the umbrella quoins alongside `burn_host_singularity` and `bus_keys_operator_owned`.

3. **Section rename + intro reframe** (line 1003 `=== BURN/BURP/BURW Identifier Vocabulary`):
   - Rename to `=== Jurisdiction`
   - Replace intro paragraph with one that introduces `{bus_jurisdiction}` as the umbrella, then names viceroyalty / investiture / lieutenancy as its three `{bus_jurisdictional_rung}` instances.
   - Add `[[bus_jurisdiction]]` and `[[bus_jurisdictional_rung]]` definition blocks at the top of the section, before the existing rung definitions.
   - The three existing rung definitions remain intact; lightly enrich their descriptions to say each "is a `{bus_jurisdictional_rung}` of the `{bus_jurisdiction}`."

4. **§Remote Node Access overview** (line 1564–1568): rewrite the brief paragraph to introduce `{bus_jurisdiction}` as the umbrella over both privileged- and workload-tier work on a node. Light touch — one paragraph.

5. **§Privilege Tiers opener** (line 1570–1586): adjust phrasing so "Privilege is encoded by regime, not by a field" is recast as "Privilege is encoded by jurisdictional rung" where the prose flows naturally — without rewriting the tier definitions themselves.

## Paddock edit

Add a header line above "**Three regimes (BURN/BURP/BURW):**" naming the umbrella, e.g.:

> **Jurisdiction (the BURN/BURP/BURW family):** _<one-line gloss>_

Then the existing three-regime block continues as a list of `{bus_jurisdictional_rung}` instances. (Keeps the change tight per the paddock-posture guidance — adding only the umbrella name, not a journal entry.)

## Validation

No `asciidoctor` binary is installed locally, and there's no BUS0 validator tabtarget. I'll do a grep-based structural check:
- Every new `<<anchor,...>>` reference site has its matching `[[anchor]]`
- No dangling `{attr}` after my edits (every used attribute is defined in the mapping section)
- Existing rung anchors (`burn_viceroyalty`, `burp_investiture`, `burw_lieutenancy`) and their references stay intact

Let me know if you have a specific validator I should run or if a grep-pass is acceptable.

## Out of scope (preserved per docket)

- bujn-/bujp-/bujw- minting prefixes — operator-side, not in BUS0; treated as final.
- No kindle / CLI / tabtarget changes.

---

Approve as drafted, or adjust (single quoin vs. two; section rename target; light vs. heavier prose touch on Privilege Tiers)?

message.content: What do you mean by 'rung'?

content.text:
I lifted "rung" from your own docket — line in ₢A-AAk reads "viceroyalty/investiture/lieutenancy are the addressing rungs but the umbrella is unnamed."

My read of the metaphor: three named addressing levels stacked under one jurisdiction, each at a different authority depth — viceroyalty names the node, investiture names a privileged grant *over* a viceroyalty, lieutenancy names a workload office *under* an investiture's authority. So a ladder image: three rungs, each addressing one regime, climbing in dependency from BURN up through BURW.

`bus_jurisdictional_rung` was meant to make that role-name explicit so the rung definitions can say "viceroyalty is the BURN rung of the jurisdiction" without prose footwork.

But if "rung" doesn't carry weight for you — or carries the wrong weight (rungs imply equal spacing / single climb path; a jurisdiction is more a *structure* with three identifier slots than a ladder) — I'm happy to drop the second quoin and let the rungs stay as plain prose under just `bus_jurisdiction`. Or substitute a different role-name (`bus_jurisdictional_office`? `bus_jurisdictional_seat`? `bus_jurisdictional_tier`?) if you have one in mind.

Which way do you want to go?

content: Heat ₣A- (Windows test infrastructure), pace ₢A-AAk: name "jurisdiction" as an explicit feature in BUS0 spec. Awaiting your call on whether to mint one quoin (`bus_jurisdiction`) or two (adding `bus_jurisdictional_rung`) before editing. (disable recaps in /config)

content.text:
Here's the current working set for ₣A-, split by what exists vs. what BUS0 specs but hasn't been implemented yet (the paddock's "yet-to-mint implementation paces").

## Implemented today (in `tt/`)

**Regime ops — present and validating:**
- `buw-rnl` — List BURN profiles
- `buw-rnr` — Render BURN profile (viceroyalty arg)
- `buw-rnv` — Validate BURN profile (viceroyalty arg)
- `buw-rpl` — List BURP profiles for current station user
- `buw-rpr` — Render BURP profile (investiture arg)
- `buw-rpv` — Validate BURP profile (investiture arg)

**Legacy Windows-handbook scaffolding (slated for cleanup per paddock AAf):**
- `buw-HWab.AccessBase`, `buw-HWar.AccessRemote`, `buw-HWax.AccessEntrypoints`
- `buw-HWec.EnvironmentCygwin`, `buw-HWew.EnvironmentWSL`

## Spec'd in BUS0 §Tabtarget Catalog but not yet implemented

**Lifecycle gap — BURW regime ops (none exist yet):**
- `buw-rwl` / `buw-rwr` / `buw-rwv` — list/render/validate BURW profiles (not in BUS0 catalog yet either; would mirror the `rn*`/`rp*` pattern)

**Privileged tier — garrison (per-platform node setup), 6 platforms:**
- `buw-npgl` Linux · `buw-npgm` Mac · `buw-npgc` Cygwin · `buw-npgw` WSL · `buw-npgp` PowerShell · `buw-npgx` Localhost

**Workload tier — conscript / discharge / inventory:**
- `buw-nwcl` / `buw-nwcm` / `buw-nwcc` / `buw-nwcw` / `buw-nwcp` / `buw-nwcx` — conscript per platform
- `buw-nwd` — Discharge
- `buw-nwi` — Inventory

**Workload operational:**
- `buw-nwk` — Knock · `buw-nwr` — Remote Run · `buw-nws` — Interactive Session

**Handbook:**
- `buw-hn0` — Handbook Node Top

## Verb naming worth noting

Paddock mentions a `withdraw` verb and a `vanquish` verb under the privileged tier ("garrison, withdraw, conscript-{platform}, discharge, vanquish, ..."). BUS0 §Verb Definitions today only has garrison / conscript / discharge / inventory — no withdraw, no vanquish. Either the paddock is ahead of the spec, or those are deliberate-but-unspec'd-yet additions. Not in scope for the current pace ₢A-AAk, but flagging it as drift between paddock and spec.

## Pace ₢A-AAk's relationship to this set

Spec-only, no behavior change. None of the above tabtargets get touched. The pace adds the *umbrella name* (`bus_jurisdiction`, optionally `bus_jurisdictional_rung`) so that future paces minting BURW regime ops, garrison verbs, conscript verbs, etc. can refer to "the jurisdiction this acts on" instead of repeatedly saying "the BURN/BURP/BURW family."

message.content: I now reject the notion of 'rung', and I want to shift your understanding of 'jurisdiction'.  Jurisdiction is an umbrella term for these features in BUK that are about reaching out to other nodes with known keying patterns deterministically, a way of unifying all tabtargets and specification sections concerning this coordinated feature.  Keep buw-r[np][lrv] but for other tabtargets, colophon will be buw-jXXX.  I am now strongly considering a significant simplification: extreme cutback on the 'workload' concept.  In short: no BURW files this heat, and instead our 'post setup' tabtargets just hardcode what script gets used.  We may need a tabtarget for 'reset workload user' which would destroy and recreate the workload user account so that subsequent workload related tabtargets start in a clean environment.  Thoughts?

content.text:
OK, three things on the table. Let me react to each, then surface one open question and one process note.

## Rung — dropped

Agreed. "Rung" was load-bearing only if `bus_jurisdiction` named a *vocabulary trio* (three identifier-levels in a stack). Once jurisdiction names a *BUK feature-area* — the cross-cutting "reach out to other nodes deterministically" capability — the rung framing is accidental. `bus_jurisdiction` survives as a single quoin; `bus_jurisdictional_rung` doesn't get minted.

## Jurisdiction-as-feature-area

This is cleaner than what the original docket assumed. The umbrella now spans:
- **Specification real-estate** — BUS0 §Remote Node Access (likely renames to §Jurisdiction or similar), BURN/BURP regime sections, identifier vocabulary, verb definitions, all the platform subdocs (BUSNL/BUSNM/BUSNW, BUSTG*, etc.)
- **Tabtarget territory** — `buw-r[np][lrv]` for regime config; `buw-j*` for everything else
- **Underlying regimes** — BURN (kept), BURP (kept), BURW (in question — see below)
- **Verbs** — garrison, knock, run, interactive_session, reset-workload, etc.

So `bus_jurisdiction` is the name of the *feature* this whole apparatus implements. The voicing is still `axl_voices axo_entity` (it names a coordinated capability), but its semantic weight is now "this section/colophon/regime is part of the jurisdiction feature" — a category-membership marker more than an identifier umbrella.

## `buw-j*` colophon convention

Clean. Drops the `buw-np*` / `buw-nw*` privileged-vs-workload tier split, which has been loud in the spec but only really matters if BURW lives. With BURW dying, the tier distinction collapses to "config" (`buw-r*`) vs. "operational" (`buw-j*`).

One ergonomic question: do you want sub-letter discipline within `buw-j*` so tab-completion clusters meaningfully? E.g., `buw-jg{l,m,c,w,p,x}` for garrison-per-platform, `buw-jr` for remote-run, `buw-js` for interactive-session, `buw-jk` for knock, `buw-jR` for reset-workload? Or just keep `buw-j*` flat? Flat is simpler; sub-lettered helps when there are >10 in the namespace.

## Dropping BURW — strong simplification, one open question

The win is real: a whole regime category gone, no per-station-user state files, no conscript/discharge state-tracking lifecycle, no `burw_lieutenancy` minting. The destroy-recreate "reset workload user" idiom is more honest than tracking "did I provision this?" in a state file — because the source of truth is the remote node, not the local file.

**Open question — where does workload-user identity live once BURW is gone?**

Three candidate homes I see:

1. **Hardcoded conventional names per platform**, encoded into the tabtarget. E.g., garrison-cygwin always provisions a workload user named `rbm-cyg`; `buw-jrc.RemoteCygwin` always SSHes as `rbm-cyg@${BURN_HOST}`. No per-installation flexibility, but maximum simplicity. The tabtarget filename IS the workload identity.

2. **Field(s) on BURN.** `BURN_WORKLOADS` already enumerates intended workload-account names per node. Today it's a *template* of valid lieutenancy strings; without BURW it could just *be* the canonical list of workload usernames the garrison/conscript verbs provision. Tabtargets pick from the list (by index or by suffix). `bujw-winpc-cyg` is then a node-declared workload account name, not a lieutenancy identifier.

3. **Field(s) on BURP.** Each investiture knows both its admin and its workload user. Pure per-shell on Windows means three BURPs per node — which is approximately what BURW was doing, just smeared into BURP.

Option 2 feels most consistent with "BURN names what's possible on the node, BURP grants the authority to do it" — and the Windows multi-shell case (cyg/wsl/pwsh) is genuinely a node-shape question, not a credential question.

**Question for you:** which of these do you want, or is there a fourth I'm missing? On Windows you need three shell paths to exercise the crucible (per RBSFR's fundus capability registry intent), so the workload-identity story has to handle the multi-shell case somehow.

## Process note

The current ₢A-AAk docket is now stale — it explicitly says "BURN/BURP/BURW family" and "viceroyalty / investiture / lieutenancy stand as jurisdictional rungs." Both phrases are dead. The conversation has moved from a tight spec-language pace into paddock-level redirection (drop BURW, redefine jurisdiction, recolopohn the verbs).

I'd suggest:
- **Don't touch BUS0 yet** — the shape is moving.
- **Resolve the workload-identity question** above (and the colophon-flat-vs-clustered question).
- **Then either** redocket ₢A-AAk to the new scope (likely larger — paddock + spec + reframe), **or** mint a partner pace (e.g., AAl drop-burw-from-concept) and let AAk stay narrow on the umbrella minting.

Want me to also pull up the BURW-touching files (`burw_regime.sh`, `burw_cli.sh`, etc.) to scope what would actually need to disappear if BURW gets cut, before you commit to the simplification?

lastPrompt: I now reject the notion of 'rung', and I want to shift your understanding of 'jurisdiction'.  Jurisdiction is an umbrella term for these features in BUK that are about reaching out to other nodes with…

message.content:
First, jurisdiction is absolutely not an entity.  It is logical only, has no code manifestation other than affiliation with buw-jXXX tabtargets and potentially a bujb_bash.sh/ bujb_cli.sh BCG implementation, perhaps.

Where does workload identity live?  Good question.  Do I recall that we broadened the BURP to include both an admin key as well as a workload user key?  Is that an answer in and of itself?  Picture a privileged command perhap buw-jpR for Reset Workload (though we should consider a 'cult verb' here) to destroy any existing profile and then create a fresh one.  Then buw-jwc[wcp] to issue a command file, or buw-jws to ssh as the workload, using 'command' selected by w-WSL, c-Cygwin, p-Powershell

content.text:
## Voicing — corrected

You're right, `axo_entity` was wrong. Jurisdiction has no code manifestation — it's a documentation umbrella + a colophon-affiliation marker. Cleanest treatment in BUS0: the quoin gets a definition site explaining what jurisdiction *is* (the BUK feature-area), but no AXLA voicing. AXLA's existing motifs are for code-realizable things (entities, commands, regimes, premises, tabtargets); a feature-area / namespace-marker isn't one of them. Better an unvoiced quoin than a mis-voiced one.

(If a `bujb_bash.sh` / `bujb_cli.sh` BCG module eventually lands as the operational seat for jurisdiction logic, *that* gets `axo_entity` — but the umbrella-name itself stays logical.)

## BURP broadening — your memory vs. the spec

Checked: BURP today carries admin-only fields — `BURP_NODE`, `BURP_USER`, `BURP_SSH_PUBKEY`, `BURP_KEY_FILE`, `BURP_COMMAND`. The recent reshape commit (a6a66d25) added `BURP_KEY_FILE` but did not add workload fields. So you're remembering an intent, not a landing.

**And yes — broadening BURP to also hold workload identity is the answer.** It collapses the per-investiture credential bundle into one regime: admin-credentials + workload-user-identity, both per investiture per station-user. Concrete shape (you confirm):

- `BURP_USER` — admin OS user (kept)
- `BURP_SSH_PUBKEY` — admin pubkey (kept)
- `BURP_KEY_FILE` — admin private key file (kept)
- `BURP_COMMAND` — admin SSH command= routing (kept)
- **`BURP_WORKLOAD_USER`** — workload OS user account name (new)
- **`BURP_WORKLOAD_PUBKEY`** — workload pubkey (new)
- **`BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`** — workload private key file (new)

Or, if the Reset ceremony auto-generates workload keys: drop `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE` and have it live by convention at `${BURS_KEY_DIR}/<investiture>-workload`. Open question; I lean toward explicit field for parity with the admin side and to keep the `bus_keys_operator_owned` premise honest.

## Tabtarget map — sub-lettered j-tree

Reading your proposal as a sub-letter discipline within `buw-j*`:

| Colophon | Tier | Verb | Shell selector | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `buw-jpR` | `p` privileged | `R` reset | — | Reset workload account (destroy + recreate) |
| `buw-jwcw` | `w` workload | `c` command-file | `w` WSL | Run command file as workload, WSL shell |
| `buw-jwcc` | `w` workload | `c` command-file | `c` Cygwin | …Cygwin |
| `buw-jwcp` | `w` workload | `c` command-file | `p` PowerShell | …PowerShell |
| `buw-jws` | `w` workload | `s` ssh-interactive | — | Interactive SSH as workload |

Two open questions:

1. **`buw-jws` shell selector** — symmetric with command-file (`buw-jwsw` / `jwsc` / `jwsp`), or implicit (one workload shell per Windows node, picked by node convention)? Symmetric is more honest; the user wants to debug each shell variant.

2. **Linux/Mac case** — `buw-jwc` and `buw-jws` with no flavor suffix? Or `buw-jwcl` / `jwcm` for Linux/Mac to keep the tree uniform? I lean uniform — `buw-jwcl`, `jwcm`, `jwcc`, `jwcw`, `jwcp` covers all five workload shells. Garrison-platform (`buw-jpgl/m/c/w/p`) gets the same treatment.

## Cult verb for Reset

Existing vocabulary: garrison (establish privileged trust), conscript (create workload user), discharge (remove workload user), inventory, knock; paddock also names withdraw, vanquish.

Reset = destroy-existing-and-conscript-fresh in one ceremony. Candidates:

- **Reconscript** — direct compound; "re-" is unambiguous here, fits the conscript register. Letter `R` works as `buw-jpR`.
- **Recommission** — military register, but commission is for officers, conscript is for draftees; workload accounts are ephemeral draftees → conscript/reconscript fits better.
- **Reincarnate** — death + rebirth; vivid but mixes religious metaphor into the colonial/military register.
- **Refound** — rebuild from foundation; less specific to the user-account ceremony.

My pick: **Reconscript**. Single word, lineage-clear, no register-mixing.

## Two questions to nail before I touch anything

1. **BURP shape** — confirm the three new workload fields (or counter-propose).
2. **Shell-selector uniformity** — `buw-jws` flat vs. `buw-jws[lmcwp]` uniform with command-file?

After those, I'll write up the redocketed ₢A-AAk (or split into AAk umbrella-naming + AAl burp-broaden + AAm tabtarget-recolophon — your call on granularity) and present the new shape before any spec edits.

message.content: Do we really need to keep handles to the private key files here?  Also I do think for clarity we need _PRIVILEGED for all the regime variables related to the priviledgedc profile

content.text:
## Private key handles — drop them

Yes, drop them. Three reasons:

1. **The current `BURP_KEY_FILE` is already an override hatch.** Spec says it's optional with empty defaulting to the investiture name. The convention IS the answer; the field exists only for the rare "my keys live somewhere weird" case that hasn't been exercised. Pay-as-you-go: add the override field if and when an operator actually needs it.

2. **Premise alignment.** `bus_keys_operator_owned` says the operator owns key layout. Having the regime carry privkey paths is a soft contradiction — the regime is leaning into key administration, not staying out of it. Dropping the field tightens the premise: regime carries pubkey (durable, cross-machine artifact) and identity (user/command); operator-owned privkey lives at a conventional path on the station, owned and named by the operator's `BURS_KEY_DIR` discipline.

3. **Validation surface shrinks.** One less field to validate-exists, one less drift point.

**Convention for privkey paths** (now load-bearing since the field is gone):
- `${BURS_KEY_DIR}/<investiture>-privileged` — admin private key
- `${BURS_KEY_DIR}/<investiture>-workload` — workload private key

Symmetric, parallels the `_PRIVILEGED` / `_WORKLOAD` field naming. The Reset/Reconscript ceremony emits both pubkey lines into BURP and tells the operator where to drop the matching privkey files.

## `_PRIVILEGED` rename — yes, with symmetric shape

Final BURP fieldset:

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `BURP_NODE` | viceroyalty reference (regime-level, tier-neutral) |
| `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` | admin OS user |
| `BURP_PRIVILEGED_SSH_PUBKEY` | admin pubkey |
| `BURP_PRIVILEGED_COMMAND` | admin SSH `command=` routing (admin-shell selection on Windows) |
| `BURP_WORKLOAD_USER` | workload OS user |
| `BURP_WORKLOAD_SSH_PUBKEY` | workload pubkey |
| `BURP_WORKLOAD_COMMAND` | workload SSH `command=` routing (workload-shell selection — `w/c/p`) |

Quoins follow: `burp_privileged_user`, `burp_privileged_ssh_pubkey`, `burp_privileged_command`, `burp_workload_user`, `burp_workload_ssh_pubkey`, `burp_workload_command`.

`BURP_NODE` stays unsuffixed because the node reference applies to both halves of the investiture — the privileged credential and the workload identity both target the same viceroyalty.

## One question before I write it up

Do you want the `_COMMAND` fields to be required, or optional-with-empty-default-for-direct-shell? Today `BURP_COMMAND` is optional. Symmetric workload-side `BURP_WORKLOAD_COMMAND` would also be optional — empty for Linux/Mac (default workload login shell), required-with-shell-selector on Windows (one of the cygwin/wsl/powershell command strings). Same answer for both fields, or different?

After that, I'll draft the redocketed ₢A-AAk (or AAk-split) shape with all three things in one place: jurisdiction-as-feature umbrella, BURW dropped, BURP broadened with `_PRIVILEGED`/`_WORKLOAD`, `buw-j*` colophon tree, Reconscript verb, dropped privkey fields. Then you approve before any BUS0 edits land.

message.content:
just require _COMMAND.  I want to force bash even for macos and linux.  Say, is that wise?

I confess I can't remember why we used to have the 'private part of the key' referred here, but now I wonder thqat it was useful for a setup step.  Do you see a case for this?  Oh and if we are renaming, use BURP_{PRIVILEGED,WORKLOAD}_PUBKEY instead...

content.text:
## Force bash — wise, with one refinement

Yes, wise. Reasoning:

- **BUS0 is the Bash Utilities Spec.** Forcing bash everywhere honors the premise instead of working around it per-platform. "We support nodes whose default shell is zsh" was never a design goal — Recipe Bottle assumes bash end-to-end.
- **Determinism over convention.** Workload accounts are ephemeral test users on remote hosts; they have no dev-tool dotfile rituals worth preserving. Privileged accounts are the operator acting through SSH; they want predictability more than personalization. Dropping shell-default shenanigans removes a class of "worked on Linux, mysteriously hung on Mac" failures before they happen.
- **One rule, not two.** "Always specify shell" is a simpler spec than "Windows must, Linux/Mac defaults." The Linux/Mac case currently being trivial (just `/bin/bash`) is exactly why writing it down costs nothing.

**Refinement:** specify `bash -l` (or `bash --login`) rather than bare `bash`. Login mode sources `/etc/profile` so you get system PATH, locale, etc., without per-user rc surprises. Bare `bash` skips both, which can leave PATH minimal. So default values land like:

- `BURP_PRIVILEGED_COMMAND="/bin/bash -l"` for Linux/Mac admin
- `BURP_WORKLOAD_COMMAND="/bin/bash -l"` for Linux/Mac workload
- Windows variants stay as the cygwin/wsl/powershell command strings as today

The minor cost: nodes need bash installed. That's already a Recipe Bottle premise (alpine-with-no-bash isn't a target).

## Privkey path — couldn't find a setup case worth keeping it

I dug for one. Setup operations that touch the privkey (initial garrison authenticating with `ssh -i`, re-key rotation, "can I authenticate?" validation) all just need a *path* — and `${BURS_KEY_DIR}/${BURP_INVESTITURE}-{privileged,workload}` derives mechanically from the convention. Validation can check the file exists at the conventional path equally well.

The cases the field genuinely helps:

- Operator with a non-conventional layout (centralized `/etc/ssh/keys/team-admin`-style storage)
- Multiple investitures sharing one privkey (one operator-admin key trusted across all nodes)
- Filenames the convention can't produce (spaces, etc.)

None are happening today. None are forecast. The original spec text even describes `BURP_KEY_FILE` as an override hatch with "empty defaults to the investiture." So it was always optional infrastructure for a use case that never materialized.

**Recommend: drop it.** If a real operator with weird key layout shows up later, add `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE` / `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE` in a one-pace addition. Premature retention is the same problem as premature abstraction.

If your gut says the original purpose was something I'm not seeing, name it and I'll re-evaluate. Otherwise convention wins.

## Naming — confirmed

`BURP_PRIVILEGED_PUBKEY` and `BURP_WORKLOAD_PUBKEY`. The `SSH_` was redundant in this domain. Quoin names: `burp_privileged_pubkey`, `burp_workload_pubkey`.

## Final BURP shape

| Field | Required | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `BURP_NODE` | yes | viceroyalty reference (tier-neutral) |
| `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` | yes | admin OS user |
| `BURP_PRIVILEGED_PUBKEY` | yes | admin pubkey |
| `BURP_PRIVILEGED_COMMAND` | yes | admin SSH `command=` (Linux/Mac: `/bin/bash -l`; Windows: shell-flavor command) |
| `BURP_WORKLOAD_USER` | yes | workload OS user |
| `BURP_WORKLOAD_PUBKEY` | yes | workload pubkey |
| `BURP_WORKLOAD_COMMAND` | yes | workload SSH `command=` (same shape as privileged) |

Privkeys live by convention at `${BURS_KEY_DIR}/<investiture>-privileged` and `${BURS_KEY_DIR}/<investiture>-workload`.

---

Ready to draft the redocketed pace shape unless you have more design points to settle. Want me to write it up now (paddock + ₢A-AAk redocket as one shape, or split into AAk umbrella-name + AAl burp-broaden + AAm burw-drop + AAn tabtarget-recolophon for finer-grained tracking)?

drop localhost for now, you've repeatedly misunderstood this so we defer localhost.

Do we need platform specific garrison commands now?  Or alternatively, I wonder if my notion of 'reset workload' ought to be subsumed into 'garrison'?

content.text:
## Localhost — dropped

Confirmed. Pulled from the table.

## Per-platform garrison + reset subsumption — yes to both

These two ideas hang together cleanly. Let me unpack.

**Subsuming reset into garrison** is the right move. Reframe garrison from "establish initial trust" to "**make this investiture clean and ready**" — admin trust placed, workload account fresh. First invocation does it from scratch (password-prompt fallback if no admin trust yet); subsequent invocations re-establish admin pubkey idempotently and rebuild the workload account. One verb, one mental model, no separate "did I garrison or reset?" question. Reconscript dies.

**Per-platform garrison tabtargets** then become unnecessary too — because the platform is already in `BURN_PLATFORM` (linux/mac/windows) and the admin-shell variant is in `BURP_PRIVILEGED_COMMAND`. Garrison can dispatch internally: read regime, branch to the right ceremony. The investiture name selects everything; the formulary derives the rest.

Single tabtarget: **`buw-jpg <investiture>`**. The five `jpg{l,m,c,w,p}` variants collapse to one.

## Same logic applies to the workload-side

If garrison consolidates, the workload-side per-shell variants (`jwc[lmcwp]`, `jws[lmcwp]`) face the same question. The shell flavor is already in `BURP_WORKLOAD_COMMAND`. Single `buw-jwc <investiture> <command-file>` and `buw-jws <investiture>` derive shell from regime data the same way garrison does platform.

Trade-off:

- **Pro consolidation:** five total jurisdiction tabtargets instead of 14+. The regime data is the single source of truth for "which platforms / shells are supported"; tabtargets shouldn't duplicate that enum. CLI interface reduces to "name the investiture, name what you want to do."
- **Pro per-shell tabtargets:** discoverability — `ls tt/buw-jw*` enumerates the support matrix at the filesystem level. Wrong-shell errors fail at filename selection rather than after regime parse. Tab-completion clusters meaningfully.

I lean consolidation. The discoverability argument loses to the duplication argument once you accept that BURN_PLATFORM and BURP_*_COMMAND are the authoritative declarations.

## Resulting working set if you take the full consolidation

| Tabtarget | Args | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `buw-rnl` | — | List BURN profiles |
| `buw-rnr` | `<viceroyalty>` | Render BURN profile |
| `buw-rnv` | `<viceroyalty>` | Validate BURN profile |
| `buw-rpl` | — | List BURP profiles for current station user |
| `buw-rpr` | `<investiture>` | Render BURP profile |
| `buw-rpv` | `<investiture>` | Validate BURP profile |
| `buw-jpg` | `<investiture>` | Garrison — admin trust + workload account, idempotent |
| `buw-jwk` | `<investiture>` | Knock — probe workload reachability |
| `buw-jwc` | `<investiture> <command-file>` | Run command file as workload (shell from regime) |
| `buw-jws` | `<investiture>` | Interactive SSH as workload (shell from regime) |
| `buw-hj0` | — | Handbook jurisdiction top |

**Eleven total — six regime-config (kept) + four jurisdiction operations + one handbook.**

Open question: do you want to take the consolidation all the way (single `buw-jwc`/`buw-jws` deriving shell from regime), or keep per-shell tabtargets on the workload side for discoverability?

message.content: Well I'm not sure that we can get by with buw-jwX without c-cygwin, w-wsl, p-powershell variants.  Your thoughts?

content.text:
You're right to push back, and the disagreement reveals a question I steamrolled. The shell letter on the tabtarget is doing one of two different things, and I conflated them.

## Two interpretations

**Interpretation A — one investiture per shell.** Each Windows shell variant gets its own investiture: `bujp-winpc-cyg`, `bujp-winpc-wsl`, `bujp-winpc-pwsh`. Three garrison ceremonies, three workload accounts on the node, three pubkey pairs. The investiture *name* already selects the shell; `BURP_WORKLOAD_COMMAND` is one string per BURP.

If that's the model, then a single `buw-jwc <investiture> <file>` *can* work — but the per-shell tabtarget letter is still useful as discoverability and a fail-fast check (`buw-jwcc bujp-winpc-wsl …` errors at the tabtarget level instead of mid-dispatch).

**Interpretation B — one investiture, multiple shell entry-points.** One Windows investiture per node: `bujp-winpc`. One workload account on the node, with three SSH entry-points (three pubkey pairs, three `command=` directives in authorized_keys, or some equivalent multiplexing). `BURP_WORKLOAD_COMMAND` becomes structured for Windows — a map from shell letter (c/w/p) to command string. The tabtarget letter is the actual runtime selector: it picks which entry-point to use against the same workload account.

This interpretation matches what you wrote earlier: *"using 'command' selected by w-WSL, c-Cygwin, p-Powershell."* Reads naturally as "the letter chooses which command-flavor to dispatch with."

## Trade-off

Interpretation B is leaner on node resources (one workload user, not three) and matches a real test-parity goal: prove the same command file behaves identically whether routed through cyg, wsl, or pwsh entry-points to the same account. But BURP gets more structured — the workload command field becomes per-shell-letter for Windows, single-valued for Linux/Mac. Asymmetric regime shape.

Interpretation A keeps BURP simple (one workload command per investiture, always) at the cost of three investitures per Windows node and three workload accounts. The tabtarget letter is useful for explicitness but not load-bearing.

## My read of your intent

Your earlier framing — *"selected by w-WSL, c-Cygwin, p-Powershell"* — points at Interpretation B. The tabtarget letter is the selector. My consolidation collapsed that into "regime carries the shell" because I was reading Interpretation A.

If B is what you want, the workload-side stays per-shell:

| Tabtarget | Args | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `buw-jwcc` | `<investiture> <file>` | Command file via Cygwin entry-point |
| `buw-jwcw` | `<investiture> <file>` | …WSL |
| `buw-jwcp` | `<investiture> <file>` | …PowerShell |
| `buw-jwsc` | `<investiture>` | Interactive Cygwin |
| `buw-jwsw` | `<investiture>` | Interactive WSL |
| `buw-jwsp` | `<investiture>` | Interactive PowerShell |

Linux/Mac stay with single `buw-jwc <investiture> <file>` and `buw-jws <investiture>` because there's only one shell. Asymmetric tree, but the asymmetry tracks the actual node shape (Windows is the multi-shell platform).

And BURP grows shell-keyed workload-command fields on the Windows side — possibly:
- `BURP_WORKLOAD_COMMAND_C`
- `BURP_WORKLOAD_COMMAND_W`
- `BURP_WORKLOAD_COMMAND_P`

(or a single structured field — open question on representation).

## Question

Before I finalize the table: is Interpretation B what you want? One investiture per Windows node, multi-entry-point workload account, tabtarget letter as selector? Or am I still misreading and you want A (per-shell investitures, letter as discoverability)?

Either is workable, but BURP's shape and the garrison ceremony's complexity differ materially.
